skill.name: writing-great-skills
skill.description: Reference guidance for writing predictable AI skills, including invocation, structure, pruning, and failure modes.
skill.summary: Helps authors design clear, predictable skills for AI agents.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported Markdown backticks as external command execution and prose formatting as weak cryptography. Review found only documentation content in SKILL.md and GLOSSARY.md, with no executable code, shell execution, network access, credential handling, or prompt injection attempt.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: The external command findings are Markdown code spans and links, such as file names and configuration terms. No shell execution syntax or runnable script is present at the cited locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is plain Markdown documentation, not Ruby or shell code. The files contain no executable command blocks or scripts.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Weak Cryptography Terms
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings are matches inside prose about skill design concepts. No cryptographic API, hashing function, key handling, or security-sensitive implementation appears in the cited files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected files are prose-only references about skill writing. No evidence found of cryptographic code or weak algorithm usage.

content.value_statement: Skill authors often struggle to make AI skills predictable, concise, and easy to invoke. This skill gives vocabulary and design rules for improving skill structure, descriptions, references, and failure modes.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to make a skill model-invoked or user-invoked.
Defines a shared vocabulary for skill design and evaluation.
Guides authors on descriptions, leading words, and trigger phrasing.
Shows how to move reference material behind context pointers.
Identifies common skill failure modes such as sprawl and duplication.
Supports pruning skills for relevance, clarity, and maintainability.
limitations: It is a writing reference, not an automated skill validator.
It does not generate complete skill packages by itself.
It does not include executable tooling or test scripts.
Its advice is conceptual and requires author judgment.

use_cases.title: Refine an Existing Skill
use_cases.description: Use the vocabulary and failure modes to find unclear triggers, duplicated guidance, and stale instructions.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Skill
use_cases.description: Decide whether the skill should be model-invoked, user-invoked, or split into smaller related skills.

use_cases.title: Review Marketplace Submissions
use_cases.description: Apply consistent concepts when assessing skill descriptions, structure, references, and documentation quality.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Skill Clarity
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one skill file
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-great-skills to review this skill for clear purpose, invocation style, and obvious duplication. Give a short list of improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Invocation Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-great-skills to evaluate whether this skill should be model-invoked or user-invoked. Recommend description changes and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Reorganize References
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-great-skills to inspect this skill for context load, progressive disclosure, and co-location problems. Suggest a revised structure.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Skill Failure Modes
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced maintenance review across related skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Use writing-great-skills to review these related skills for sprawl, sediment, duplication, weak leading words, and premature completion risk.

output_examples.input: A skill description lists many similar trigger phrases for the same task.
output_examples.output: The review recommends collapsing repeated trigger phrases into one clear branch and using a stronger leading word.

output_examples.input: A long skill mixes core steps with definitions and background material.
output_examples.output: The review separates required steps from reference material and suggests moving optional definitions behind context pointers.

output_examples.input: A team wants consistent terms for discussing skill quality.
output_examples.output: The response explains terms such as context load, duplication, sprawl, and completion criterion in practical review language.

best_practices: Use the glossary terms consistently when reviewing or editing a skill.
Evaluate invocation style before rewriting the body of a skill.
Move reference material only when a clear context pointer remains.
anti_patterns: Do not keep repeated trigger phrases that describe the same branch.
Do not hide material that every run of the skill must read.
Do not treat the glossary as a replacement for testing skill behavior.

faq.question: What does this skill help me do?
faq.answer: It helps you write and revise AI skills so their invocation, structure, and guidance are more predictable.

faq.question: Does it create a full skill automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference guidance that an author or agent can apply during skill writing.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why does it include a glossary?
faq.answer: The glossary defines the terms used to evaluate skill quality, structure, invocation, and failure modes.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed files are documentation only, and no executable or network behavior was found.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Skill authors, reviewers, and teams that maintain AI agent workflows can use it to improve skill quality.
